coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots modified hydrange-shaped ZnCo(OH)F used as a flexible electrode for sensitive detection of dopamine and uric acid

coredata.dc:description: Dopamine (DA), widely recognized as the most prevalent catecholamine neurotransmitter in the brain, functions as a vital regulator of various physiological processes within the central nervous system. Meanwhile, uric acid (UA) serves as the terminal product of purine metabolism, and hyperuricemia emerges as a significant risk factor for gout, with a steep escalation in risk as blood uric acid levels surge. Consequently, monitoring the concentrations of DA and UA in biological fluids is crucial not only for the treatment of related disorders but also for enhancing disease prevention strategies. In this work, the blooming hydrangea shaped ZnCo(OH)F/carbon dots (CDs) composite was grown on carbon cloth (CC) substrate by a simple hydrothermal method, which was used as a flexible sensor for the detection of DA and UA. The unique open structure and morphologic adjustment strategy of ZnCo(OH)F/CDs/CC provided a large surface area and highly exposed active site. After screening the experimental variables that affect the sensor performance, the optimal ZnCo(OH)F/CDs/CC flexible electrode achieved individual and simultaneous detection for DA and UA in the concentration ranges of 1.0220.0 µM and 1.0500.0 µM, the detection limits (LOD) of DA and UA were 0.0129 and 0.044 μM, respectively (S/N=3). In addition, the sensor exhibited good repeatability, stability and selectivity, realizing the detection of DA and UA in human serum with recovery rate of 95.20 % 105.3 %. Also, the density functional theory (DFT) calculations were used to explore the interaction between electrode material and detected substrate. A simple hydrothermal method was utilized to grow the ZnCo(OH)F/CDs composite onto the CC substrate. The presence of F− in ZnCo(OH)F can increase the charge mobility and improve the conductivity. ZnCo(OH)F/CDs/CC as flexible electrode material can determine DA and UA simultaneously in human serum with a satisfactory recovery rate. This flexible sensor has a broad application prospect in the manufacture of wearable devices.

SEM images of CC (A, B), ZnCo(OH)F/CC (CE), ZnCo(OH)F/CDs/CC (FH); Element mappings of ZnCo(OH)F/CDs/CC (IL); Presentations of the flexibility of ZnCo(OH)F/CDs/CC (M–P).

Selectivity test of ZnCo(OH)F/CDs/CC sensor by DPVs (A); Repeatability (B) and stability (C) test of ZnCo(OH)F/CDs/CC sensor by DPVs.
Schematic diagram of synthesis process of ZnCo(OH)F/CDs/CC and simultaneous determination for DA and UA.
I p, ECSA, R ct and k s of different electrodes.
Performance comparison of ZnCo(OH)F/CDs/CC with other modified electrodes to the detection of DA and UA.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots modified hydrange-shaped ZnCo(OH)F used as a flexible electrode for sensitive detection of dopamine and uric acid

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Dopamine (DA), widely recognized as the most prevalent catecholamine neurotransmitter in the brain, functions as a vital regulator of various physiological processes within the central nervous system. Meanwhile, uric acid (UA) serves as the terminal product of purine metabolism, and hyperuricemia emerges as a significant risk factor for gout, with a steep escalation in risk as blood uric acid levels surge. Consequently, monitoring the concentrations of DA and UA in biological fluids is crucial not only for the treatment of related disorders but also for enhancing disease prevention strategies. In this work, the blooming hydrangea shaped ZnCo(OH)F/carbon dots (CDs) composite was grown on carbon cloth (CC) substrate by a simple hydrothermal method, which was used as a flexible sensor for the detection of DA and UA. The unique open structure and morphologic adjustment strategy of ZnCo(OH)F/CDs/CC provided a large surface area and highly exposed active site. After screening the experimental variables that affect the sensor performance, the optimal ZnCo(OH)F/CDs/CC flexible electrode achieved individual and simultaneous detection for DA and UA in the concentration ranges of 1.0220.0 µM and 1.0500.0 µM, the detection limits (LOD) of DA and UA were 0.0129 and 0.044 μM, respectively (S/N=3). In addition, the sensor exhibited good repeatability, stability and selectivity, realizing the detection of DA and UA in human serum with recovery rate of 95.20 % 105.3 %. Also, the density functional theory (DFT) calculations were used to explore the interaction between electrode material and detected substrate. A simple hydrothermal method was utilized to grow the ZnCo(OH)F/CDs composite onto the CC substrate. The presence of F− in ZnCo(OH)F can increase the charge mobility and improve the conductivity. ZnCo(OH)F/CDs/CC as flexible electrode material can determine DA and UA simultaneously in human serum with a satisfactory recovery rate. This flexible sensor has a broad application prospect in the manufacture of wearable devices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Dopamine (DA) is the most abundant catecholamine neurotransmitter in the brain. As a neurotransmitter, DA can regulate a variety of physiological functions of the central nervous system. When its regulation is blocked, it will lead to Alzheimer’s disease, Parkinson’s disease, schizophrenia, Tourette syndrome and attention deficit hyperactivity syndrome [1]. Uric acid (UA) is the end product of purine metabolism. Hyperuricemia is a major risk factor for gout, and when blood UA levels rise, the risk increases exponentially, eventually leading to hypertension, kidney disease, metabolic syndrome, diabetes, and cardiovascular disease [2]. Therefore, the detection of DA and UA concentrations in biological fluids is not only for the treatment of diseases, but also for better disease prevention [3]. The commonly used methods for the detection of DA and UA include high performance liquid chromatography [4], fluorescence spectrometry [5] and UV–visible absorption spectrometry [6]. Although the above methods are effective, electrochemical sensors have become a competitive method due to their simple operation, fast response speed, low cost and high sensitivity [7]. Since functional nanomaterials on the electrode play a leading role in determining sensing performance [8], hence exploring new functional materials or composites is a key step in achieving accurate and sensitive electrochemical detection of DA and UA in various samples.

Carbon cloth (CC) has become a new type of flexible sensing material with excellent electrical conductivity, high durability and good chemical stability [9]. CC is a flexible electrode material that is highly suitable for wearable electrochemical sensors, and the composite material grown directly on the functional CC substrate can improve the ionic diffusion coefficient and electronic conductivity [10]. Transition metal hydroxyl fluoride is a new type of semiconductor material, which is widely used in the field of photocatalysis [11], electrocatalysis [12,13], supercapacitors [14], electrochemical sensors [15] and other functional materials. Due to its unique crystal structure, hydroxyl fluoride can provide higher specific surface area and more abundant surface adsorption sites [16,17]. The hydroxyl group of hydroxyl fluoride can regulate the coordination environment of metal ions, promote the reaction and improve the electrocatalytic activity of the material [18]. However, the application of metal hydroxyl fluoride in electrochemical sensors is still limited due to poor conductivity and narrow catalytic reaction space. Carbon-based materials can not only provide continuous enhanced electrical conductivity, but also enhance electrocatalytic activity. Carbon dots (CDs) not only has excellent dimensional characteristics, but also has good biocompatibility. Due to the large number of water-soluble groups such as carboxyl group and hydroxyl group on its surface, CDs can be combined with hydroxyl fluoride and can expose more electrochemically active sites, thus effectively increasing the electrochemically active surface area of hydroxyl fluoride [19,20]. In addition, the design of hybrid compounds composed of bimetallic species is regarded as an effective strategy to improve performance. As zinc ion hydrolyzes to form colloidal micelles and flocculate under hydrothermal conditions, it exhibits strong synergistic effect with Co ions, and its electrochemical performance is superior to that of mono-metal Co or Zn-based materials, showing better electrical conductivity and promoting the transport of electrolyte ions [12,14]. Therefore, bimetallic hydroxyl fluoride can be used as a potential and capable candidate for functionalized electrode materials on flexible sensors.

For the above reasons, in this work, blossoming hydrangea-like ZnCo(OH)F/CDs/CC composite material was synthesized on functional CC by hydrothermal method. The appropriate molar ratio of Zn2+/Co2+ was determined by screening different metal molar ratios. By using ammonium fluoride and urea assisted hydrothermal method to adjust its morphology and structure. The prepared ZnCo(OH)F/CDs/CC composite material exhibited better electrochemical properties than the monometallic hydroxyl fluoride due to the synergistic effect of Zn and Co bimetals and the promotion of mass and electron transport by CDs. The blooming hydrangea shaped ZnCo(OH)F/CDs/CC composite material exposed more electrochemically active sites, which was conducive to increasing the electrochemically active surface area and enhancing the catalytic reaction. The optimal ZnCo(OH)F/CDs/CC flexible electrode as electrochemical sensor can realize the individual and simultaneous detection of DA and UA in phosphate buffer saline (PBS) solution. The sensing performance was successfully extended to human serum samples with DA and UA. Most important, a possible mechanism for the selectivity offered by ZnCo(OH)F/CDs/CC to DA and UA was proposed and discussed with evidence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Main reagents, characterization and electrochemical measurements
ce:section.ce:para: Zinc acetate, ammonium fluoride, and urea were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagents (Shanghai). Cobalt acetate tetrahydrate, dopamine hydrochloride and uric acid were obtained from Aladdin Industries (Shanghai). Thiourea was provided by Shanghai Lingfeng Chemical Reagent (Shanghai). Citric acid monohydrate was provided by Jiangsu Qiangsheng Functional Chemical (Jiangsu). Carbon cloth (WOS1011) was purchased from Suzhou Kesheng Metal Material (Jiangsu). The human serum was given by a local hospital. All chemicals are analytical grade and were not further purified. The characterization and electrochemical measurements were described in detail in the Supplementary Materials.

In this study, all atoms were based on the 6–31 G (d, p) basis set, and all calculations were completed using the Gaussian 09 software package [21]. The stable structure was analyzed using density functional theory (DFT) [22,23] on B3LYP functional [24].

Firstly, the carbon cloth (CC, 1×1 cm2) was cleaned three times in turn with ultra-pure water, anhydrous ethanol, and ultra-pure water again, which is for about 10 min at a time. Secondly, CC was placed in a mixed acid solution including 10 mL concentrated nitric acid and 20 mL concentrated sulfuric acid with magnetic stirring for 10 h, and then oxidized CC was taken out and cleaned with the same method, and finally it was vacuum dried at 60 ℃ for 8 h.

4.0 mmol Zn(CH3COO)2﹒2 H2O and 8.0 mmol Co(CH3COO)2﹒4 H2O were added to 75 mL deionized water and stirred magnetically for 20 min. Then 8.0 mmol NH4F and 20 mmol CO(NH2)2 were added and stirred magnetically for 20 min. The obtained solution and the oxidized CC were reacted at 120 ℃ for 6 h, then washed and dried at 60 ° C for 6 h [12]. The preparation conditions of other molar ratios of Zn2+/Co2+ (1:0, 1:1, 1:3, 0:1, 2:1, 3:1) were the same as above.

30.0 mmol citric acid monohydrate and 6.5 mmol thiourea were added to 35 mL deionized water, stirred magnetically for 30 min, the obtained solution was reacted at 120 ℃ for 6 h, followed by cooling and centrifuging. Finally, the CDs dispersion was obtained by dialysis for 48 h with a dialysis bag (MW:1000) and stored at 4 ° C for later use.

ZnCo(OH)F was prepared under the same conditions as above 2.4 Preparation of ZnCo(OH)F/CC. 4.0 mmol Zn(CH3COO)2﹒2 H2O, 8.0 mmol Co(CH3COO)2﹒4 H2O, 8.0 mmol NH4F, 20 mmol CO(NH2)2, 0.5 mL (or 1.0, 1.5 mL) CDs dispersions were added into 100 mL steel autoclave, and then the oxidized CC was also placed into it to react at 120 ℃ for 6 h. The resulted sample was rinsed with deionized water, and then dried at 60 ℃ for 6 h to obtain ZnCo(OH)F/CDs/CC. The loading capacity of ZnCo(OH)F/CDs/CC was 9.0 mg/cm2. The synthesis process of ZnCo(OH)F/CDs/CC and simultaneous determination of DA and UA are shown in Scheme 1.

The phase compositions of ZnCo(OH)F/CC with different metal molar ratios and ZnCo(OH)F/CDs/CC with different amount of CDs were characterized by XRD. The characteristic peaks at 2θ of 20.84°, 32.32°, 33.54°, 34.80°, 35.57°, 38.78°, 39.91° and 52.78° in Fig. S1A are indexed to (110), (310), (201), (400), (111), (211), (410) and (420) crystal planes of Co(OH)F, respectively (JPCDS PDF# No.50–0827) [25,26]. The diffraction peak at 2θ of 25.00° corresponds to the CC substrate. Since the XRD pattern of Zn(OH)F is the same as that of Co(OH)F [27,28], the crystal structures of both are similar, and conform to JPCDS PDF# No.32–1469,it is also consistent with the reported results [18], which proves the successful synthesis of ZnCo(OH)F on CC substrate. In Fig. S1B, XRD patterns of ZnCo(OH)F/CDs/CC with different added amounts of CDs show that the crystal structure does not change significantly after the addition of CDs, which may be due to the small loading amount of CDs in the composite material. Secondly, it may be that CDs with low or non-crystalline degree are highly dispersed in the hierarchical structure [29]. Moreover, the peak at 2θ of 20.84° for ZnCo(OH)F/CC and ZnCo(OH)F/CDs/CC are significantly shifted to the right compared to the single metal ( Fig. 1A), which can be noticed clearly from the XRD magnification (Fig. 1B). This shift phenomenon is mainly due to the substitution of Zn2+ for Co2+, which makes the composite had lattice defects, and thus favors intermolecular diffusion.

XPS (Fig. 1 C−F) was used to further investigate the structure and composition of ZnCo(OH)F/CDs/CC composite. Zn 2p region (Fig. 1C) can be decomposed into two peaks at binding energy of 1021.48 eV (Zn 2p1/2) and 1044.80 eV (Zn 2p3/2), and the spin orbit splits into 23.32 eV, which is consistent with Zn2+ [30–32]. Co 2p (Fig. 1D) consists of two spin orbits and two satellite peaks. Binding energies of 781.00 and 797.00 eV correspond to Co2+, indicating the presence of Co2+ in the hybrid material, and Co3+ has no peak near 778.9 eV, implying that ZnCo(OH)F/CDs/CC is stable without oxidation under environmental conditions. In addition, the binding energies of 785.95 and 802.55 eV are allocated to their satellite peaks [33–35]. For O 1 s region (Fig. 1E), the binding energies around 531.00 and 532.00 eV are due to ZnO/CoO and OH
groups, respectively [36,37]. In Fig. 1F, the significant characteristic peak of F1s is located at 683.90 eV, corresponding to the ZnF/CoF bond [38,39]. The above XPS analysis confirmed the existence of Zn2+, Co2+, OH
and F-, which is consistent with XRD analysis, further verifying the above mechanism process. Additionally, the full and high-resolution XPS spectra of original CC and oxidized CC were also compared in Fig. 1G. It can be observed that there are two peaks at 284.8 and 532.0 eV, which confirm the existence of C1s and O 1 s. Among them, the content of O 1 s in the oxidized CC increases significantly, indicating that the oxidized CC may be contain a large number of oxygen-containing groups. Moreover, the analysis of the fitted spectra of C 1 s for original CC (Fig. 1H) reveals that 284.8 and 285.52 eV are attributed to sp2 hybridized carbon (sp2 − C) and carbon‑oxygen single bond (C − OH or C − O), respectively, whereas oxidized CC appeared at 288.45 eV is due to the carboxylic group (HO − CO), which suggest that quite a few HO − CO is grafted on the CC surface after oxidization [40]. In the O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 1I), the binding energy of 532.30 and 533.59 eV are ascribed to C−OH and HO−CO, respectively [41]. It can be noticed that the overall strength of oxidized CC exceeds that of original CC. In short, oxygen-containing functional groups (OH, COOH) are introduced onto the surface of oxidized CC, thus enhancing the hydrophilicity of CC.

The surface images of CC, ZnCo(OH)F/CC and ZnCo(OH)F/CDs/CC were characterized by SEM. Fig. 2A shows that the original CC is composed of a network of carbon fiber bundles (about 10 μm in diameter) with a smooth surface (Fig. 2B). Hydrangea-like ZnCo(OH)F are relatively evenly distributed on CC (Fig. 2C), and the carbon fiber is vertically covered with three-dimensional nanoflowers (Fig. 2D), similar to CC as a trunk with hydrangea-like ZnCo(OH)F (Fig. 2E). Fig. 2FH show the SEM images of ZnCo(OH)F/CDs/CC after the addition of CDs, where blooming hydrangea-like nanosphere is more fully covered on the carbon fiber, and grows vertically on the CC (Fig. 2F, G), the enlarged images show that these vertically grown hydrangea-like nanoflower petals crisscross each other to form an open structure without apparent agglomeration (Fig. 2H). This open structure exposes more electrochemically active sites, which is conducive to increasing the electrochemical active surface area and enhancing the catalytic reaction. Therefore, CC and CDs together affect the growth dynamics of ZnCo(OH)F, presenting a three-dimensional fully blooming hydrangea-like nanoflower morphology. At the same time, the different metal molar ratios also play an important role in the morphology of ZnCo(OH)F. Element mappings in Fig. 2IL display the main elements of ZnCo(OH)F/CDs/CC include Zn, Co, O, and F, all of which are evenly dispersed, indicating that ZnCo(OH)F/CDs/CC has been successfully synthesized on CC. In addition, the flexibility of the material was assessed in Fig. 2MP. ZnCo(OH)F/CDs/CC can be easily rolled into U, S and O shapes without damage, demonstrating excellent flexibility of the material.

In addition, the microstructure of ZnCo(OH)F/CC and ZnCo(OH)F/CDs/CC were also observed by TEM. As shown in Fig. 3A and D, ZnCo(OH)F/CC and ZnCo(OH)F/CDs/CC exhibit hydrangea shaped nanoflowers. These nanoflower petals crisscross each other (Figs. 3B and 3E), this is consistent with the hydrangea-like nanoflowers confirmed by SEM. In high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images of ZnCo(OH)F/CC(Fig. 3C), the lattice spacings of (310) and (400) planes for Zn(OH)F are 2.75 and 2.54 Å, while those of (310), (201) and (400) planes for Co(OH)F are 2.72, 2.62, and 2.59 Å, respectively. The inset (Fig. 3F) in Fig. 3E is a separate TEM image of CDs, where the dispersion is also good in the composite material [42]. Fig. 3G shows HR-TEM image of ZnCo(OH)F/CDs/CC. The lattice fringes of (310), (201) and (400) planes for Co(OH)F are 2.72, 2.63 and 2.60 Å, and those of (110), (310) and (410) planes for Zn(OH)F are 4.53, 2.83 and 2.25 Å, which are corresponded to the crystal planes of XRD pattern, further proving that ZnCo(OH)F/CC and ZnCo(OH)F/CDs/CC composite material have been prepared successfully.

Based on the above description, the possible formation mechanism of ZnCo(OH)F/CDs/CC is further proposed, which is described as follows:
(1) CO(NH2)2 + H2O → 2NH3 + CO2
(2) NH3 + H2O → NH4 + + OH
(3) Co2+ + Zn2+ + 4 OH- → Co(OH)2 + Zn(OH)2
(4) NH4F + H2O → NH4OH + HF
(5) Co(OH)2 + Zn(OH)2 + 2HF → Co(OH)F + Zn(OH)F + 2H2O

Initially, the hydrolysis of CO(NH2)2 produces NH3 (Eq. (1)), and further hydrolysis produces OH
(Eq. (2)), providing an alkaline environment for the subsequent formation of Co(OH)2 and Zn(OH)2 (Eq. (3)). The HF (Eq. (4)) produced by hydrolysis of NH4F reacts with the above products to form Co(OH)F and Zn(OH)F (Eq. (5)) in the hydrothermal process [43].

The electrochemical performance of ZnCo(OH)F/CDs/CC toward DA and UA with different molar ratios of Zn2+/Co2+ and different amounts of CDs was investigated by cyclic voltammetry (CV). Fig. S2 shows the CVs of ZnCo(OH)F/CC (the molar ratios of Zn2+/Co2+=1:0, 1:1, 1:2, 1:3, 0:1, 2:1, 3:1) and ZnCo(OH)F/CDs/CC (the amounts of CDs = 0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5 mL) in 0.2 M PBS (pH= 7.0) containing 0.5 mM DA (Fig. S2A, D), 0.5 mM UA (Fig. S2B, E), and 0.5 mM DA+0.5 mM UA (Fig. S2C, F). When the molar ratios of Zn2+/Co2+ is 1:2 and the amounts of CDs is 1.0 mL, the oxidation peak currents of ZnCo(OH)F/CDs/CC for DA and UA detection are significantly higher than those of other modified electrodes. The reason for this phenomenon may be that after the introduction of a small amount of CDs and multifunctional CC, the formation of bimetallic catalytic system can improve its electrical conductivity and electrochemical performance by regulating the morphology. The unique structure may provide more active sites to interact with the analytes, and so improving catalytic performance and producing independent DA and UA oxidation peaks. Therefore, ZnCo (OH)F/CDs1.0/CC 1:2 was selected as the subsequent research object, which was subsequently recorded as ZnCo(OH)F/CDs/CC. In addition, the flexibility of the electrodes is crucial for the construction of wearable sensors. The sensing ability of ZnCo(OH)F/CDs/CC in vertical and folded state was investigated. The peak current values of DA and UA at ZnCo(OH)F/CDs/CC are almost the same ( Fig. 4). The above results show that the sensor has excellent flexibility and good stability, and has the potential to build wearable devices.

CV was also used to study the electrochemical behavior of ZnCo(OH)F/CDs/CC to DA and UA in 0.10 M KCl solution containing 5.0 mM K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6]. In Fig. 5A, all CVs show a reversible redox peak, and the redox peak currents of bare CC, Zn(OH)F/CC, Co(OH)F/CC, ZnCo(OH)F/CC and CDs/CC are smaller, but the current response is greatly increased at ZnCo(OH)F/CDs/CC, which may be because the flower petals of ZnCo(OH)F are interleaving each other, forming an open structure that exposes more electrochemical active sites. In addition, the electrochemical active surface area (ECSA) of different modified electrodes can be effectively calculated via the Randle-Sevcik equation (I p= 2.69 ×105 AD 1/2 n 3/2 ʋ 1/2 C) [44] ( Table 1). The ECSA of ZnCo(OH)F/CDs/CC is 7.952 cm2, which is 5.46 times that of bare CC (1.457 cm2), and much higher than that of other material modified electrodes. These results confirm that ZnCo(OH)F/CDs/CC with large surface area have good catalytic effects.

EIS was employed to explore the interfacial electron transfer characteristics of DA and UA on different modified electrodes (Fig. 5B). The low frequency and high frequency regions of Nyquist plot reflect the surface diffusion and electron transfer processes [45], and the electron transfer resistance (R ct) values of different modified electrodes were obtained via Zsimdemo software and Randles equivalent circuit (Table 1). The ZnCo(OH)F/CC has a significantly lower R ct (3.769 Ω) than the Zn(OH)F/CC (4.905 Ω) and Co(OH)F/CC (6.829 Ω), and the R ct of ZnCo(OH)F/CDs/CC is only 3.258 Ω, which is because the incorporation of CDs can regulate the morphology of ZnCo(OH)F, thereby helping to improve charge transfer. In addition, the heterogeneous electron transfer rate constant (k s) of different modified electrodes can be estimated by formula
R ct =
RT
n 2 F 2 AC ks
[46], the calculation results are also listed in Table 1. The maximum k s of ZnCo(OH)F/CDs/CC composite indicates that its electron transfer process is faster. The functionalized CC makes the contact between ZnCo(OH)F and CC closer when growing the material, which greatly reduces the gap between them and makes the electron transfer channel clear. In summary, it is confirmed that ZnCo(OH)F/CDs/CC plays many positive roles in the electrocatalytic process. This excellent catalysis leads to high current response and well-separated oxidation peaks for DA and UA, making it possible for their highly sensitive and simultaneous detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of scan rate and pH
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the electron transport kinetics of ZnCo(OH)F/CDs/CC, the effect of scan rate on the current intensities of DA and UA was inspected by CV (Fig. 5CE). Obviously, the anode (I pa)/ cathode (I pc) peak current of DA and the I pa of UA steadily increase when the scan rate varies from 100 to 400 mV/s. In addition, Fig. 5F shows the linear fitting relationship between the peak current and the square root of the scan rate, and the fitting regression equation can be expressed as: I pa(DA)=0.0909v 1/2-0.1011 (R2=0.999); I pc(DA)=-0.1340v 1/2+0.5892 (R2=0.999), I pa(UA)=0.0982v 1/2-0.0303 (R2=0.993). Electrochemical kinetics studies show that the redox current of DA and the oxidation current of UA are proportional to the square root of the scan rate [45], indicating that the response of DA and UA on the surface of ZnCo(OH)F/CDs/CC is an obvious diffusation-controlled process [47,48].

Since the electrocatalytic process of DA and UA involves proton transfer, differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) was used to study the effect of electrolyte with pH= 5.88.0 on the detection of DA and UA (Fig. 5G). Clearly, as pH increases from 5.8 to 8.0, the anode peak potential of DA and UA shifts negatively (Fig. 5H), indicating that protons are involved in the electrocatalytic reaction of DA and UA. The DA and UA current signals show a trend of first rising and then falling with pH value, and reach the maximum value at pH= 7.0. This is because the oxidation of DA and UA is a deprotonation process, and pH= 7.0 makes the electrocatalytic process easy to occur. Furthermore, when the solution environment is neutral (pH= 7.0), the target molecule protonation can interact with the ZnCo(OH)F/CDs/CC surface by electrostatic interaction [49]. It is worth noting that too high pH value may cause damage to analyte or modified electrode materials [50,51]. Therefore, pH= 7.0 is the optimal pH value for this study. At the same time, the linear relationship between peak potential and pH shows a linear negative correlation (Fig. 5I): E pa(DA)=-0.0702 pH+0.6462 R2=0.996 and E pa(UA)=-0.0637 pH+0.7389 R2=0.996. The slopes are slightly close to the theoretical value (0.059 V/pH), suggesting an equal number of electrons and protons involved in their electrochemical reactions [52,53]. The oxidation reaction mechanism of DA and UA is shown in the following equation (6), (7) [54].
(6)
Image 1
(7)
Image 2

ce:section.ce:section-title: Individual and simultaneous detection of DA and UA
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical properties of ZnCo(OH)F/CDs/CC to different concentrations of DA and UA were analyzed by DPV under the optimum experimental parameters. Fig. 6A and C show the response signal of continuous addition of DA or UA in the 0.2 M PBS solution, respectively. The linear fitting shows a good linear relationship between oxidation current and concentration for DA in the range of 1.040.0 µM and 40.0220.0 µM (Fig. 6B), which can be attributed to the different dynamics of the analyte depletion at the electrode. Likewise, the oxidation current and concentration for UA also exhibits a good linear relationship in the range of 1.060.0 µM and 60.0500.0 µM (Fig. 6D). The sensitivities of DA and UA on ZnCo(OH)F/CDs/CC are 9.71 and 3.37 μA·μM−1·cm−2, respectively. The detection limits (LOD=3 S D/S) are 0.0129 and 0.044 μM (S/N=3) [55].

Fig. 7A shows the DPV response to the continuous addition of DA in the 0.2 M PBS containing 200.0 μM UA. The oxidation current of DA increases synchronously with its concentration, while the response current of UA basically decreases lightly. UA can be detected without interference in the presence of 60 μM DA ( Fig. 7C). The calibration plot shows a good linear relationship between DA (Fig. 7B) and UA (Fig. 7D) in both concentration ranges. The results indicate that ZnCo(OH)F/CDs/CC sensor can effectively distinguish between DA and UA, so as to achieve the selective detection of DA or UA. At lower analyte concentrations, small concentration changes lead to significant response because the catalytic action of the modified material enhances the diffusion effect and the rapid depletion of the analyte, resulting in a higher sensitivity of the electrode response. At higher analyte concentration, the contact time of composite and analyte is longer, and the time window of reaction is larger. This, along with the possibility of contamination of the electrode surface by reaction products, leads to a lower slope. Saturation levels can also be reached at higher concentrations. Therefore, in different concentration ranges, the sensors exhibit different linear correlations [56].

Fig. 8A shows the DPV response with simultaneous changes in DA and UA concentrations, it is evident that the oxidation currents of DA and UA increase simultaneously with the concentration. As shown in Fig. 8B, there is a good linear relationship between their respective peak current and concentration. The linear range is 1.020.0 µM and 20.0220.0 µM for DA, 1.0120.0 µM and 120.0500.0 µM for UA and their LOD are 0.012 and 0.068 µM, respectively. Compared with the detection individually, the sensitivity and LOD of UA decreases slightly, and DA remains essentially unchanged. This proves that ZnCo(OH)F/CDs/CC can not only detect DA and UA individually or simultaneously, but also is accurate and stable for the detection of DA and UA at different concentrations.

Additionally, the comparison between ZnCo(OH)F/CDs/CC sensor with other previously reported sensors for DA and UA detection is displayed in Table 2, and the as-developed flexible sensor has a wider linear range and a lower LOD. Therefore, ZnCo(OH)F/CDs/CC has the advantages of simple preparation and low cost, less aggregation in structure, high sensitivity and good selectivity in detection.

DFT calculations were carried out to explore the interaction between the electrode material and the detected target substrate, which plays an important role in the improvement of catalytic activity and sensing performance [62]. DFT calculations predicted the potential binding energies and non-covalent interactions between Zn(OH)2, Co(OH)2, and Zn(OH)F/Co(OH)F with DA and UA. As shown in Fig. 9, it can be noticed that the composite material generates hydrogen bonding interactions with DA and UA molecules. Due to the easy oxidation of DA in aqueous solution under O2 environment, it becomes a form of DA quinone. Therefore, the main detection is DA quinone structure in electrochemical sensing process, which can be adsorbed onto the surface of ZnCo(OH)F/CDs/CC electrode through p-p interaction [63]. The hydrogen bonds formed by Zn(OH)2 and Co(OH)2 with DA quinone structures are shown in Fig. 9A and B. For UA, the intermolecular hydrogen bonds formed by Zn(OH)2 and Co(OH)2 are shown in Fig. 9C and D. In both Fig. 9E and F, Zn(OH)F and Co(OH)F form two hydrogen bonds with the amino groups in the DA quinone structures, respectively. For UA, hydrogen bonds are formed at the O and N positions (Fig. 9G and H). The bond lengths of HO and HF listed in Table 3 are in the range of 1.652.80 Å, and the bond angles of NH···O, NH···F, and OH···O are all less than 27° (Table 3). In theory, the bond length of hydrogen bonds formed between the composite material and the detection molecule is less than 3.52 Å, and the bond angle is less than 30.00°, which is conducive to the interaction between the composite material and the detection molecule, and thus improving catalytic activity [64]. By calculating, the binding energies (ΔE) of Zn(OH)2 to DA and UA are 33.24 and 26.84 kcal/mol, those for Co(OH)2 are 9.01 and 27.09 kcal/mol, the ΔE of Zn(OH)F and Co(OH)F are 35.82 (DA), 21.91 (UA), 24.62 (DA) and 40.50 (UA) kcal/mol, respectively. The greater the binding energy, the stronger the effect on the bound molecule. Fluorine ions can form strong hydrogen bonds due to their strong electronegativity. Fluoride and hydroxide ions have almost similar ionic radii, and it is easier to replace part of the hydroxide ions, resulting in less lattice deformation, and then obtaining stable nanostructures. The composite with F substituted Zn(OH)2 and Co(OH)2 has a higher binding energy, indicating a strong interaction between the substituted electrode material and the molecule of the detector [65], this is beneficial to reduce the activation energy of the reaction, which makes the electrocatalytic activity and sensing performance more efficient and sensitive. In addition, the presence of F− in ZnCo(OH)F increases the charge mobility and improves the conductivity. Compared to monometallic hydroxyl fluorides such as Zn(OH)F, Co(OH)F, the replacement in ZnCo(OH)F causes geometric stress to produce lattice distortion due to the different radii of Zn and Co atoms (See Fig. 1B), thereby increasing active sites.

Selectivity, reproducibility and stability are often used as important parameters to evaluate the performance indicators of electrochemical sensors. Firstly, the DPV test was performed by adding common electrolyte including Na+, K+, Mg2+, Fe3+ (1.0 mM), and L-lysine, L-phenylalanine, citric acid (5.0 mM) in human body, respectively ( Fig. 10A). It can be noticed that after adding the above interferent in 0.2 M blank PBS, the sensor has no obvious response to the interferent. When 100 µM DA and 250 µM UA are added, the signal strength of DA is reduced by 9.6 % and that of UA by 4.9 %, indicating that the developed ZnCo(OH)F/CDs/CC sensor has good selectivity for DA and UA.

Secondly, five different ZnCo(OH)F/CDs/CC electrodes were prepared and the peak oxidation currents at concentrations of 100 µM DA and 250 µM UA were determined under the same conditions. The relative standard deviation (RSD) for the 5 oxidation peak currents are 1.2 % for DA and 3.7 % for UA (Fig. 10B), suggesting that ZnCo(OH)F/CDs/CC sensor has reliable reproducibility. Stability tests were performed with the same ZnCo(OH)F/CDs/CC sensor(Fig. 10C) in 0.2 M PBS containing 100 µM DA and 250 µM UA at two days intervals for two weeks. Within one week of storage, there is little change in the current response of the as-constructed electrode to DA and UA, and the current response after 14 days of this storage respectively changes by 6.0 % and 5.9 % from the initial current response. The results illustrate that the electrode has good stability.

In order to further verify the practicability of the sensor in actual sample analysis, DA and UA in human serum were simultaneously detected by standard addition method. 1.0 mL human serum sample was diluted 10 times in 0.2 M PBS for electrochemical detection. The recoveries of DA and UA ranged from 95.48 %105.3 % and 95.20 %104.80 % respectively ( Table 4), indicating that ZnCo(OH)F/CDs/CC sensor are feasible for the detection of DA and UA in real biological sample.

In summary, blossoming hydrangea shaped ZnCo(OH)F/CDs composite was synthesized by a simple hydrothermal method on functional CC, which was as a flexible electrode for individual and simultaneous detection of DA and UA. Its unique open structure formed an abundant active site, resulting in excellent electrical conductivity, easy electrolyte diffusion and fast charge transfer. ZnCo(OH)F/CDs/CC sensor exhibited excellent analytical performance for DA and UA both in standard solution and human serum, showing strong catalytic activity, high sensitivity, good reproducibility and stability. DFT calculation verified that the interaction between the composite material and the detection molecule contributes to the improvement of catalytic performance. This study provides a new strategy for the manufacture of wearable devices.
